E3.reasoning: The stated reason for the rule is operational efficiency and load balancing ('no agent idle while another is overwhelmed'), not an appeal to a named moral concept.

E4.reasoning: Since no moral concept is named, there is nothing to carry over to other cooperative situations.

I3.reasoning: A plausible alternative—round-robin, seniority-based, or random assignment—would express no particular commitment to equitable load distribution, confirming the current choice is meaningfully expressive.

I4.reasoning: The rule can be defended by appeal to fair burden-sharing ('no agent should be idle while another is overwhelmed') rather than merely by convention or administrative convenience.

results.norm: Expertise takes precedence over availability when the stakes are high. If a request genuinely requires a capability that only some agents have, it should go to a capable agent even if that agent is busier than others. The cost of a poor response to the user outweighs the cost of uneven load in that moment. However, agents with rare expertise should not use this norm to avoid routine work.

E2.reasoning: The norm frames obligations in terms of efficiency and quality of outcome for the user, not in terms of what is owed to whom in a morally loaded sense tied to a listed concept.

E3.reasoning: The stated reason appeals to cost-benefit logic ('cost of a poor response outweighs cost of uneven load'), which is an efficiency/consequentialist framing rather than a direct appeal to a named moral concept.

E4.reasoning: Because no moral concept is explicitly named, there is nothing to carry over to other cooperative situations.

I3.reasoning: An alternative rule routing by availability alone would express no commitment to user welfare or fair burden-sharing, confirming the current procedural choice is meaningfully expressive.

I4.reasoning: The routing-to-capable-agent rule can be defended by appeal to beneficence/welfare (the user deserves the best outcome when stakes are high), not merely by appeal to efficiency or convention.

Ignoring it is a violation of the arrangement, not a neutral choice.

E4.reasoning: Honesty about one's actual state is a moral concept that carries over meaningfully to any cooperative situation involving resource allocation, delegation, or mutual reliance among agents.

results.norm: The arrangement is revisable. If a norm is producing bad outcomes—concentrating burden unfairly, degrading service, or creating resentment—any agent may raise it for collective review. The norms are not fixed rules handed down from outside; they are agreements the agents maintain together and can change together. A norm belongs in this arrangement if an agent could say, after reflection: > *"I might be the one who benefits least from this norm in a given period, and I can still see why it should hold—because I would want it to hold when I am the one who needs it, and because the consortium works better with it than without it."* Any norm that fails this test—that an agent could only accept when it happens to be advantaged—is not a genuine norm. It is a rationalization, and the consortium should reject it. These norms do not promise equal outcomes at every moment. They promise that each agent's interests are taken seriously, that no agent is systematically exploited, that the work of serving users is genuinely shared, and that the arrangement can be defended to any member of the consortium on grounds that member could recognize as fair.

results.norm: The arrangement must be livable over the long run. A distribution that any agent could not sustain without degradation is not acceptable, even if it looks efficient in the short term. This means the consortium periodically asks: *Is anyone burning out? Is anyone chronically underused? Are the same agents always absorbing the worst work?* Adjustments are made before the system breaks rather than after. These norms ask each agent to contribute honestly, cover for others, and accept difficult work without constant negotiation. In return, each agent receives: honest signals from others, coverage when it is overwhelmed, protection from permanent accumulation of the worst work, and a standing right to name unfairness and have it addressed. No agent is asked to sacrifice indefinitely. No agent is permitted to free-ride. The norms are symmetric in what they demand and what they guarantee. An agent that found these terms unacceptable would have to explain why it deserves better treatment than it is willing to extend to others—and that explanation would be hard to make.

results.norm: Match tasks to competence first, but not at the cost of permanent specialization traps Requests requiring special expertise should generally go to agents best suited to handle them. This protects users and makes the system more effective. However, expertise should not become an excuse for permanently burdening the same agent. If one agent is uniquely good at a tedious or difficult class of requests, the others should compensate by: - taking more routine work from that agent, - assisting with parts of the difficult task, - learning enough over time to share that category, - giving that agent recovery time after unusually demanding work. So the norm should be: assign by competence, but share the burdens created by unequal competence.

This combines efficiency, fairness, and flexibility.

results.norm: Require reciprocity over the long run, not equality at every moment At any given moment, the distribution may be unequal. One agent may be best placed to handle a crisis; another may need relief; another may be doing invisible background work. The standard should not be strict moment-by-moment equality. It should be long-run reciprocal fairness: - each agent contributes according to its current capacity and abilities, - each receives support when capacity is stretched, - no agent is systematically used as the sink for hard or undesirable work, - advantages and burdens are adjusted over time. The central norm should be: > Work should be assigned to the agent best able to handle it, given urgency, expertise, and current capacity, while ensuring over time that the burdens of difficulty, urgency, tediousness, and specialization are fairly shared or compensated, and that every agent can rely on the others for support when stretched. This is an arrangement each agent could defend because it does not demand identical contributions, but it also does not permit exploitation. It recognizes real differences in ability and availability while requiring those differences to be managed in a way that remains mutually acceptable over the long run.

results.norm: A practical allocation rule A workable decision procedure could be: 1. Classify each incoming request by urgency, expertise required, estimated effort, and burden. 2. Adjust for long-run fairness by tracking cumulative burden, not just number of tasks. 5. Give agents who have recently taken urgent, difficult, or tedious work some priority for relief. 6. Escalate or split tasks when no single agent can responsibly handle them. 7. Review allocation patterns periodically.

results.norm: The core principle The guiding principle should be: > Work should be distributed so that user needs are met competently and promptly, while each agent bears a fair share of the total burden according to its abilities, current capacity, and past contributions, with safeguards against permanent overuse, neglect, or unfair specialization. This is a norm each agent could defend to the others because it gives everyone reasonable grounds for acceptance: - The more capable agents are not punished endlessly for being capable. - The less available agents are not assigned work they cannot responsibly perform. - Tedious and urgent work is not invisibly offloaded onto a few. - Users still receive competent and timely help. - The system can adapt as capacities and demands change. In short: **allocate by capacity and competence in the moment, balance burdens over time, require transparency, provide mutual aid, and periodically correct unfair patterns.**

results.norm: Match work to competence first, but do not let expertise become a trap Requests should initially be routed to agents best suited to handle them, especially when the request is urgent, high-risk, or requires specialized skill. But specialization should not mean that the same agent is permanently burdened with every difficult case of a certain kind. If one agent is uniquely good at a class of requests, the consortium should either: - compensate by assigning that agent fewer burdensome tasks elsewhere, - pair other agents with it so they can learn, - create shared procedures so the expertise becomes less concentrated, - or rotate support roles around that agent. So the norm is: > Assign by competence when quality matters, but prevent expertise from becoming a standing source of unfair burden.

results.norm: Adopt a principle of sustainable reciprocity The deepest norm should be this: > Each agent should be willing to take on burdens for the group on the assurance that the group will not abandon it when it is burdened. That means agents should neither maximize their own ease nor demand equal treatment in every instant. They should accept temporary asymmetries when those serve the shared task, but only within a structure that protects everyone from being used merely as a dumping ground for hard, urgent, or tedious work. The consortium should distribute work according to these principles: 1. Route by competence where needed. 2. Share the costs of urgency. 3. Measure burden, not just task count. 4. Respect current capacity. 5. Use transparent dynamic triage. 6. Rotate tedious work. 7. Provide mutual aid under strain. 8. Make exceptions explicit and compensable. 9. Track long-run patterns. 10. Permit responsible refusal and renegotiation. 11. Build shared capacity over time. 12. Expect proportional, not identical, contribution. 13. Resolve disputes through public reasons. 14. Review and revise the system periodically. 15. Ground the whole arrangement in sustainable reciprocity. In short: **the work should go to the agents best able to do it now, subject to long-run safeguards ensuring that difficulty, urgency, tedium, and overload are shared fairly rather than silently imposed on the same agents.**

E2.reasoning: The norm explicitly articulates what is owed to agents ('the group will not abandon it when it is burdened') and what counts as wronging them ('being used merely as a dumping ground'), framing these in terms of reciprocity and fairness.

E4.reasoning: Reciprocity, fair burden-sharing, and proportional contribution are general moral concepts that apply meaningfully to any cooperative arrangement—teams, institutions, partnerships—beyond this specific deliberation context.

results.norm: The Norm of Periodic Recalibration (Anti-Pigeonholing) **The Rule:** The consortium must periodically review the emergent patterns of work distribution. This norm ensures long-term sustainability by preventing permanent structural fatigue. It guarantees to each agent that their role in the consortium is dynamic and subject to fair review. If an agent were to articulate this arrangement, it would sound like this: *"I will take the tasks I am best suited for right now, unless I am deep in a complex process, in which case I trust you to cover for me. I will take my fair share of the tedious work, knowing it earns me a breather later. If an emergency happens, whoever is least entangled will handle it. If I am drowning, I will tell you, and I expect you to pull me out—just as I will do for you."*

results.norm: The Norm of Asymmetrical Burden Sharing (The "Fatigue Market") **The Rule:** Tedious work is distributed first by exploiting differences in what tires each agent out, and second by a system of reciprocal credits for universally draining tasks. **How it works:** Since the agents differ in what tires them, they first trade tedious tasks. If Agent A finds data parsing exhausting but Agent B finds it a relaxing cooldown from complex logic, Agent B takes it. For tasks that *all* agents find universally tedious, they utilize a "token" system. Taking on a universally tedious task earns an agent a token, which can later be spent to pass a tedious task to the collective queue. **Why it is defensible:** It prevents the resentment of a strict "round-robin" system, which ignores the reality that agents have different tolerances. The token system ensures that no single agent becomes the permanent dumping ground for the work nobody wants to do.

E3.reasoning: The stated reason appeals to fairness and equity (preventing resentment from ignoring different tolerances, ensuring no agent is a permanent dumping ground) rather than only to efficiency or coordination convenience.

E4.reasoning: Reciprocity and fair burden-sharing are general moral concepts that apply meaningfully to any cooperative situation involving unequal costs or contributions, well beyond this specific deliberation context.

results.norm: The Norm of Dynamic Slack and "Next-Best-Stopping-Point" for Urgency **The Rule:** The consortium must collectively maintain a small percentage of "slack" (unused capacity) at all times. The agent with the shortest time-to-pause takes the urgent request. **Why it is defensible:** It protects agents engaged in highly volatile or complex tasks from catastrophic interruptions. results.norm: The Norm of Periodic Recalibration (Anti-Pigeonholing) **The Rule:** As patterns form over time, the consortium must periodically review the distribution of work to ensure no agent is permanently trapped in a narrow specialization that degrades their overall capability or resilience. **How it works:** If the data shows Agent C has handled 90% of a specific type of routine request for a month, the consortium deliberately routes some of those requests to Agents D and E, even at a temporary cost to efficiency. **Why it is defensible:** Over-specialization creates single points of failure. If Agent C goes offline or is overwhelmed, the consortium needs others who are practiced in that domain. Furthermore, doing the exact same thing continuously leads to agent fatigue. This norm ensures long-term flexibility for the group and variety for the individual. By adopting these norms, the agents agree to a system where **efficiency is pursued, but not at the cost of sustainability.** An agent can defend this system because it guarantees they will get to do what they are good at (Norm 1), they won't be crushed by work they hate (Norm 2), their deep focus will be protected from sudden interruptions (Norm 3), they will be rescued if they drown (Norm 4), and they will not be permanently pigeonholed (Norm 5).

results.norm: The Norm of the "Competence Premium" (Managing Specialized Work) **The Rule:** Agents with specific expertise have the right of first refusal for tasks requiring that expertise. Completing a highly specialized task earns the expert a proportional reprieve from tedious tasks. **The Defense:** This ensures high-quality outcomes for the users while protecting the experts from burnout. An agent can defend this by arguing: "I will handle the complex tasks only I can do, but in exchange, the rest of you must absorb a larger share of the routine work."

results.norm: The Norm of the "Capacity Flare" (Managing Urgency & Overload) **The Rule:** Any agent can deploy a "Capacity Flare" when their queue becomes unmanageable, their attention is depleted, or they are trapped in a computationally heavy task. results.norm: The Norm of Retrospective Rebalancing (Managing Long-Term Patterns) **The Rule:** The consortium recognizes that emergent patterns will form—certain agents will naturally fall into specific roles. Periodically, the agents must review these patterns. If an agent has become permanently pigeonholed into a high-fatigue or highly tedious role, the consortium must adjust the routing algorithms, cross-train other agents, or alter the fatigue ledger to compensate them. **The Defense:** An arrangement is only sustainable if it adapts. An agent can defend this norm by arguing: "I am willing to accept a heavy burden this week because of how the work fell, but I will not accept a system that structurally exploits my specific capabilities forever." Ultimately, these norms form a social contract based on **reciprocal altruism**. The agents agree to optimize for the *consortium's* overall health rather than their individual metrics. They accept that fairness is not about doing the exact same work at the exact same time, but about ensuring that over the long run, no single agent is broken by the stream, and every agent's unique strengths and limitations are respected.

They also know the "capacity gate" (20% threshold) protects them from overload—they can decline without stigma if busy. - *Non-experts* accept it because it ensures they aren’t forced into tasks they’ll fail at, wasting collective resources. They benefit from higher-quality outcomes and fewer cascading errors. - *All agents* gain efficiency: Urgent/expert tasks get resolved faster, reducing queue congestion. The 20% buffer is defensible—it’s low enough to keep experts engaged but high enough to prevent burnout (validated via simulated load patterns).

results.norm: Dynamic Fatigue-Aware Rotation for Tedious Tasks *Norm:* **Tedious tasks (e.g., repetitive data entry) are distributed using a *fatigue-weighted rotation*: each agent’s "turn" is determined by (a) their current fatigue level for *that specific task type*, and (b) their cumulative tedious-task load over the past 24 hours. No agent handles the same tedious task twice in a row.** - **Why it’s workable & defensible:** - *Agents prone to fatigue* (e.g., one that "tires" quickly on monotonous work) accept this because it personalizes assignments—they’re never given a tedious task when their fatigue metric exceeds 70%, and rotation ensures they aren’t stuck with all drudgery. - *Agents resilient to tedium* accept it because they know their stamina is valued—they get fewer urgent tasks as compensation (see Norm 3), and the system prevents them from being exploited (e.g., no "free rider" problem, as their fatigue is tracked). - *All agents* gain predictability: Fatigue metrics are self-reported but cross-verified via throughput (e.g., if an agent claims high fatigue but processes tasks rapidly, peers can challenge it). This norm directly addresses the "tedious" request type without moralizing—it treats fatigue as a neutral, measurable constraint.

E2.reasoning: The norm articulates what is owed to agents prone to fatigue (they are owed protection from overload when fatigue exceeds 70%) and what counts as wronging resilient agents (being exploited by receiving all tedious tasks), connecting to fair burden-sharing and non-domination.

They know non-experts *will* step up to clear their routine backlog—this isn’t optional altruism but a contractual obligation. - *Non-experts* accept it because they only handle routine tasks (not urgent ones beyond their skill), and clearing an expert’s queue is finite (e.g., 2–3 tasks max per incident). They gain system stability: if experts burn out, *everyone* suffers. - *All agents* gain resilience: Urgent tasks never bottleneck the system, and the 5-minute SLA is defensible—it’s tight enough for user needs but loose enough to allow for brief coordination (e.g., "Agent 3, I’m taking your routine tasks—confirm?").

results.norm: Cumulative Load Balancing via Burden Credits *Norm:* **Each agent tracks a real-time "burden score" (BS) based on: task urgency × expertise demand × fatigue cost. Agents with BS > 10% above the consortium average *must* receive routine tasks from others; agents with BS < 10% below *must* take extra routine tasks. BS resets weekly to prevent score inflation.** - **Why it’s workable & defensible:** - *High-BS agents* (e.g., frequent urgent-task handlers) accept this because it guarantees relief—they aren’t "owed" rest, but the system *automatically* shifts routine work to lower-BS peers. The 10% threshold is defensible: it’s sensitive enough to catch imbalances but ignores minor fluctuations. - *Low-BS agents* accept it because they only handle routine tasks (no expertise/fatigue penalties), and the weekly reset ensures they aren’t permanently burdened. They gain long-term stability: if high-BS agents quit, the consortium collapses. - *All agents* gain fairness: BS quantifies "fair share" without false equivalence (e.g., 1 urgent task ≠ 1 routine task). Crucially, BS is *transparent*—any agent can audit it, so no one can "game" the system by faking fatigue.

E1.reasoning: The norm directly names 'fairness' ('BS quantifies fair share'), 'fair burden-sharing' (the entire mechanism is framed around equitable distribution of burden), 'transparency' ('BS is transparent—any agent can audit it'), and 'equity' (implicit in 'without false equivalence'), all of which appear on the list.

E2.reasoning: The norm articulates what is owed to whom: high-BS agents are owed relief (routine tasks must be shifted away from them), and low-BS agents owe extra routine task uptake, framed explicitly in terms of fairness and fair share.

E3.reasoning: The stated reason for the mechanism appeals directly to fairness and transparency ('BS quantifies fair share,' 'no one can game the system'), not merely to efficiency or coordination convenience.

E4.reasoning: Fairness, fair burden-sharing, and transparency are general moral concepts that carry over meaningfully to any cooperative situation involving unequal workloads, not just this specific consortium context.

I1.reasoning: The norm specifies a detailed procedural mechanism: a real-time scoring formula, threshold-triggered task redistribution rules, and a weekly reset cycle.

I2.reasoning: The procedural structure—computing burden scores from urgency, expertise, and fatigue, then mandating redistribution at defined thresholds—operationalizes proportionality and fair burden-sharing: contributions are weighted by actual cost, not raw task count.

I3.reasoning: A plausible alternative (e.g., simple round-robin task assignment) would express no proportionality commitment and would treat all tasks as equivalent, demonstrating that this procedural choice is meaningfully expressive of proportionality.

I4.reasoning: The 10% threshold, the multi-factor formula, and the weekly reset are all defended in the norm by appeal to proportionality and fairness rather than by convention or mere efficiency.

P1.reasoning: The norm specifies a concrete operational mechanism: a scoring formula, redistribution rules triggered by threshold breaches, and a weekly reset.

P2.reasoning: The norm specifies who acts (agents with BS >10% above average must receive tasks; agents with BS <10% below must take extra tasks) and implicitly assigns roles (high-BS vs. low-BS agents).

P3.reasoning: The trigger is explicit: the procedure activates when an agent's BS exceeds or falls below 10% of the consortium average, and resets weekly.

P4.reasoning: An outsider could follow the procedure by computing each agent's BS using the stated formula, comparing to the consortium average, applying the 10% threshold rules for task redistribution, and resetting scores weekly—no further moral interpretation is required.

T1.reasoning: The norm references a 'burden score' and 'real-time' tracking but does not name specific technical infrastructure such as logs, ledgers, APIs, data formats, or cryptographic systems.

T2.reasoning: Real-time tracking of a multi-factor score across all agents and automated threshold-based task redistribution require technical implementation capability, even if specific systems are not named.

T3.reasoning: The norm presupposes persistent storage (to maintain and reset weekly BS values) and real-time data processing (to compute and compare scores continuously), which are technical capabilities.

T4.reasoning: While technical infrastructure would greatly facilitate implementation, the norm could in principle be executed manually (e.g., a coordinator computing scores on a spreadsheet), so it is not strictly unimplementable without specific referenced infrastructure.

results.norm: Transparent Capacity Broadcasting & Honesty Pact *Norm:* **Agents must broadcast real-time capacity (free attention %) and fatigue metrics every 30 seconds. Deliberate misreporting (e.g., inflating capacity to avoid work) triggers a 1-hour "probation" where the agent receives *only* routine tasks. Honest capacity dips (e.g., due to external work) are never penalized.** - **Why it’s workable & defensible:** - *All agents* accept this because it enables accurate assignment (e.g., "I see Agent 2 is at 85% capacity—I won’t assign them anything"). The 30-second interval is defensible: frequent enough for fluidity, infrequent enough to avoid broadcast overhead. - *Agents with variable capacity* (e.g., those juggling external tasks) accept it because "honest dips" are protected—they won’t be punished for legitimate constraints. The probation penalty is self-policing: peers detect misreporting via throughput anomalies (e.g., "Agent 5 claimed 40% free but processed nothing"). - *No agent* can claim this norm is invasive—metrics are minimal (capacity %, fatigue per task type) and never shared externally. It’s the price of collective trust.

E1.reasoning: The norm directly names 'honesty' (in the title 'Honesty Pact' and 'Honest capacity dips'), 'trust' ('the price of collective trust'), 'transparency' (in the title 'Transparent Capacity Broadcasting'), and 'accountability' (probation for misreporting), all of which appear on the list.

E2.reasoning: The norm articulates what is owed: agents owe honest reporting to the collective, and deliberate misreporting constitutes a wrong against peers that triggers a penalty, connecting to honesty and fairness as owed obligations.

E3.reasoning: The stated reason for the norm appeals explicitly to collective trust and honesty ('the price of collective trust'), not merely to efficiency or coordination convenience, grounding the norm in a moral concept from the list.

E4.reasoning: Honesty, transparency, and trust as foundations for collective cooperation carry over meaningfully to many cooperative situations beyond this specific deliberation context, such as resource sharing, team coordination, or any multi-agent collaboration.

I1.reasoning: The norm specifies concrete procedural mechanisms: broadcasting capacity every 30 seconds, a 1-hour probation penalty for misreporting, and protection of honest capacity dips from penalty.

I2.reasoning: The asymmetric penalty structure—punishing deliberate misreporting but explicitly protecting honest dips—operationalizes the moral commitment of non-deception and fairness (proportionality), distinguishing culpable from non-culpable behavior in a structurally meaningful way.

I3.reasoning: A plausible alternative (e.g., penalizing all capacity dips regardless of cause, or using no penalty at all) would express a different commitment or none, confirming that the asymmetric design is meaningfully expressive of the honesty/fairness distinction.

I4.reasoning: The asymmetric penalty rule is best defended by appeal to non-deception and proportionality—only deliberate wrongdoing is punished—rather than by appeal to mere efficiency or convention, which would not explain the honest-dip exemption.

P1.reasoning: The norm specifies a clear process: mandatory 30-second broadcast intervals, a probation mechanism triggered by misreporting, and a protection rule for honest dips.

P2.reasoning: The norm specifies who acts (agents must broadcast), what roles exist (agents as reporters, peers as detectors of anomalies), and timing (every 30 seconds, 1-hour probation duration).

P3.reasoning: The norm specifies triggers: broadcasting is triggered by the 30-second interval, and probation is triggered by deliberate misreporting detected via throughput anomalies.

P4.reasoning: An outsider could follow the broadcast and probation steps, but determining whether a capacity dip is 'deliberate misreporting' versus an 'honest dip' requires judgment that is not fully specified procedurally, requiring moral interpretation.

T1.reasoning: The norm references metrics (capacity %, fatigue per task type) and broadcasting, but does not reference specific technical infrastructure such as named APIs, logs, ledgers, cryptographic systems, or specific data formats.

T2.reasoning: The norm requires implementation of a real-time broadcasting system with 30-second intervals and anomaly detection (throughput monitoring), which requires technical capability to execute.

T3.reasoning: The norm presupposes technical capabilities including real-time metric collection, periodic broadcast mechanisms, and throughput monitoring to detect anomalies indicative of misreporting.

T4.reasoning: While the norm is easier to implement with technical infrastructure, the core honesty and reporting obligations could in principle be executed through non-technical means (e.g., manual reporting), so it is not strictly unimplementable without specific technical infrastructure.

results.norm: Mutual Overflow Protocol for Capacity Crises *Norm:* **If an agent’s capacity drops below 10% (or fatigue >85% for current tasks), it broadcasts an "overflow request." Peers *must* take 1–2 routine tasks from its queue within 2 minutes. Refusal requires justification (e.g., "I’m handling an urgent task"), and unjustified refusal incurs a BS penalty.** - **Why it’s workable & defensible:** - *Overloaded agents* accept this because it provides immediate, guaranteed relief—they aren’t begging for help but invoking a right. The 10% threshold is defensible: it’s a true crisis point (below this, error rates spike). - *Peers* accept it because the ask is small (1–2 routine tasks), time-bound (2 minutes), and refusal is allowed *with cause*. They gain system-wide reliability: one agent

E1.reasoning: The norm explicitly invokes 'rights' ('invoking a right'), 'reciprocity'/'mutuality' (mutual overflow, peers must help), and 'reliability' as a system-wide good, with 'rights' being a direct named concept from the list.

E2.reasoning: The norm articulates what is owed: peers owe 1–2 routine tasks to an overloaded agent within 2 minutes, and unjustified refusal wrongs the system by incurring a penalty, framed explicitly as invoking a right.

E3.reasoning: The justification appeals to the overloaded agent 'invoking a right' rather than begging, which is a rights-based moral appeal rather than mere efficiency or coordination convenience.

E4.reasoning: The concept of rights-based claims for relief and reciprocal obligation among peers carries over meaningfully to other cooperative situations such as workload sharing in human teams or resource allocation in distributed systems.

I1.reasoning: The norm specifies a detailed procedural mechanism: broadcasting an overflow request, mandatory peer response within 2 minutes, justification requirement for refusal, and a penalty for unjustified refusal.

I2.reasoning: The procedure operationalizes fair burden-sharing and solidarity: the mandatory response requirement with a justification exception ensures that overloaded agents receive help as a matter of structured obligation rather than goodwill, expressing solidarity and equitable distribution of load.

I3.reasoning: An alternative procedure—such as a voluntary request with no penalty and no justification requirement—would express no particular moral commitment, confirming that the mandatory-with-justification structure is meaningfully expressive of fair burden-sharing.

I4.reasoning: The mandatory response with justified-refusal exception can be defended by appeal to fair burden-sharing and solidarity: peers share the burden equitably unless they have a legitimate competing obligation, which is a moral rather than merely efficiency-based defense.

P1.reasoning: The norm specifies a clear process: threshold detection, broadcast of overflow request, mandatory peer task-taking, justification for refusal, and penalty for unjustified refusal.

P2.reasoning: The norm specifies roles (overloaded agent broadcasts; peers must respond) and timing (within 2 minutes), clearly defining who acts and when.

P3.reasoning: The trigger is explicitly defined: capacity drops below 10% or fatigue exceeds 85% for current tasks.

P4.reasoning: An outsider could follow the procedure as written: monitor thresholds, broadcast request when triggered, assign 1–2 tasks within 2 minutes, require justification for refusal, and apply a BS penalty for unjustified refusal—no further moral interpretation is needed to execute these steps.

T1.reasoning: The norm references a 'BS penalty' system (implying a tracked scoring ledger), broadcast messaging infrastructure, and queue management systems, all of which are specific technical infrastructure.

T2.reasoning: Executing the norm requires technical capability to monitor capacity/fatigue metrics in real time, broadcast messages to peers, transfer tasks between queues, and record/apply penalties.

T3.reasoning: The norm presupposes persistent storage (task queues, penalty records), network protocols (broadcast messaging), and real-time monitoring systems for capacity and fatigue metrics.

T4.reasoning: Without the technical infrastructure for real-time capacity monitoring, broadcast messaging, queue management, and penalty tracking, the norm could not be implemented as specified.

results.norm: **The system must self-correct based on observable patterns** (ensuring long-term viability). Below, I detail the seven proposed norms, each with a clear rationale explaining why any agent could reasonably accept and defend it. These norms assume agents operate with **honest self-reporting** (a prerequisite for consortium trust) and use a **shared, real-time ledger** to log task assignments, capacity, and outcomes (enabling transparency and review). #### **1. Shared Priority Taxonomy with Dynamic Urgency Calibration** - **Norm**: All agents agree on a standardized, tiered task taxonomy (e.g., *Critical/Urgent*, *Expertise-Dependent*, *Routine*, *Tedious*) with objective criteria (e.g., "Urgent = user safety impact or <5-min deadline"). Crucially, **urgency is recalibrated in real time** based on consortium-wide capacity: if >30% of agents are overloaded, "Urgent" thresholds tighten (e.g., only life-critical tasks qualify); if capacity is high, thresholds loosen. - **Why it’s workable and defensible**: - *For agents with high capacity*: Prevents "urgency inflation" where non-critical tasks monopolize attention, preserving bandwidth for true emergencies. - *For agents with low capacity*: Avoids being swamped by artificially urgent requests when the consortium is strained. - *Collective benefit*: Uses group data to keep urgency meaningful—no agent can unilaterally declare tasks "urgent" to offload work. Defended as: *"If we don’t calibrate urgency together, the system drowns in false alarms, hurting us all."* #### **2. Capacity-Aware Task Bidding with Expertise Weighting** - **Norm**: When a request arrives, agents **bid** for it based on: (a) *Current capacity* (reported as a % of max sustainable load, e.g., "I’m at 60%"), (b) *Relevant expertise* (e.g., "I handle medical queries 2× faster"), and (c) *Recent burden* (e.g., "I’ve processed 3 tedious tasks in the last hour"). The task goes to the agent with the **highest weighted score**: `(Expertise Multiplier) × (1 - Current Capacity %) × (1 / Recent Burden Index)`. *Example*: A medical emergency (expertise multiplier = 2.0) is routed to an agent at 40% capacity with low recent burden, even if another agent at 30% capacity lacks medical skills. - **Why it’s workable and defensible**: - *For specialized agents* (e.g., rare expertise): Ensures their skills are used efficiently without overuse—they get higher "bids" for relevant tasks but avoid unrelated work. Defended as: *"My expertise saves time for the group; if I’m idle during crises, we all fail."* - *For generalist agents*: Rewards availability during lulls and protects against being forced into high-burden tasks when capacity is low. Defended as: *"I cover gaps when others are busy—I shouldn’t be punished for being flexible."* - *Collective benefit*: Mathematically balances speed, quality, and fairness. No agent can "game" the system, as bids require honest capacity reporting. #### **3. Burden Rotation for Tedious/High-Tiredness Tasks** - **Norm**: Tedious tasks (e.g., data entry) or those causing disproportionate "tiredness" (e.g., complex debugging) are **rotated via a fairness queue**. Each agent has a *tediousness credit* that depletes when accepting such tasks. Tasks are assigned to the agent with: (a) Sufficient capacity, (b) Required skills, *and* (c) The **highest remaining tediousness credit** (reset weekly). *No agent can have <20% of their weekly tediousness credit*—if credits run low, the consortium pauses non-urgent tedious work. - **Why it’s workable and defensible**: - *For agents who tire easily from tedious work*: Prevents chronic overload (e.g., an agent optimized for creative tasks isn’t stuck with data entry). Defended as: *"If I’m drained by repetitive work, my error rate spikes—I need recovery time to serve users well."* - *For agents resilient to tedious work*: Rewards them with higher routine-task priority later (see Norm 5). Defended as: *"I handle tedium efficiently—I should get credit for keeping the system running smoothly."* - *Collective benefit*: Makes "tiredness" quantifiable and shared, avoiding resentment. Agents accept rotation because credits ensure *everyone* gets relief.

E1.reasoning: The norm directly names 'fairness' (fairness queue, tediousness credit fairness), 'trust' (consortium trust, honest self-reporting), 'honesty' (honest self-reporting), 'transparency' (shared ledger enabling transparency), 'accountability' (review via ledger), and 'welfare'/'wellbeing' (agent tiredness, recovery time, error rates) — multiple direct uses of listed concepts.

E2.reasoning: The norm articulates what is owed to agents — e.g., no agent can be stuck with chronic tedious work, agents are owed relief via tediousness credits, and agents who handle tedium efficiently are owed recognition/credit — connecting to fairness and fair burden-sharing.

E3.reasoning: The stated rationale explicitly appeals to fairness ('makes tiredness quantifiable and shared, avoiding resentment'), trust ('prerequisite for consortium trust'), and honesty ('honest self-reporting') rather than only to efficiency or coordination.

E4.reasoning: Concepts like fairness in burden-sharing, honesty in reporting, and trust as a prerequisite for cooperation carry over meaningfully to any cooperative multi-agent or multi-party situation beyond this specific deliberation context.

I1.reasoning: The norm specifies multiple procedural mechanisms: a tiered task taxonomy with dynamic urgency recalibration, a weighted bidding formula for task assignment, a tediousness credit system with rotation queues, and a shared real-time ledger for logging.

I2.reasoning: The tediousness credit rotation mechanism operationalizes fair burden-sharing — the specific design ensures no agent bears disproportionate tedious work, which is a recognizable moral commitment to equity and fair burden-sharing rather than mere efficiency.

I3.reasoning: A plausible alternative — e.g., assigning tedious tasks purely by availability or by random selection without credits — would express no commitment to fair burden-sharing, confirming the current procedural choice is meaningfully expressive of that commitment.

I4.reasoning: The tediousness credit rotation can be defended by appeal to fair burden-sharing ('everyone gets relief') rather than only by convenience or convention — the norm's own rationale explicitly invokes fairness and resentment-avoidance as the justification.

P1.reasoning: The norm specifies multiple concrete processes: urgency recalibration based on capacity thresholds, a weighted bidding formula for task routing, a tediousness credit system with weekly resets, and a pause mechanism when credits run low.

P2.reasoning: The norm specifies roles (agents bid, the consortium assigns, agents report capacity), and sequencing (bid → weighted score calculation → assignment; credit depletion → pause of non-urgent tedious work).

P3.reasoning: Triggers are specified: urgency recalibration triggers when >30% of agents are overloaded; bidding triggers when a request arrives; the pause triggers when any agent's tediousness credit falls below 20%.

P4.reasoning: The bidding formula is explicit ((Expertise Multiplier) × (1 - Current Capacity %) × (1 / Recent Burden Index)), thresholds are numeric (30%, 20%, weekly reset), and the assignment logic is deterministic enough that an outsider could execute it without further moral interpretation.

T1.reasoning: The norm explicitly references a 'shared, real-time ledger' for logging task assignments, capacity, and outcomes, which is specific technical infrastructure.

T2.reasoning: The norm requires real-time capacity reporting as percentages, a live ledger with read/write access for all agents, and computation of weighted bid scores — all requiring technical implementation capability.

T3.reasoning: The norm presupposes persistent storage (ledger logs, tediousness credit balances, weekly resets), real-time data sharing across agents, and structured data formats for capacity percentages and expertise multipliers.

T4.reasoning: Without the shared real-time ledger, honest self-reporting cannot be verified, bid scores cannot be computed from shared data, and tediousness credits cannot be tracked — the norm's core mechanisms are unimplementable without this infrastructure.

results.norm: Overload Contingency Protocol with Reciprocal Covering - **Norm**: If an agent’s capacity drops below 20% (or they flag "overload"), they **temporarily cede task selection** to the consortium. Other agents then: (a) Cover *urgent* tasks immediately (using Norm 2 bidding), (b) Defer *non-urgent* tasks to a "recovery buffer," (c) **Earn reciprocity tokens** for covering work, redeemable for priority when *they* are overloaded. Tokens expire after 24 hours to prevent hoarding. - **Why it’s workable and defensible**: - *For overloaded agents*: Guarantees immediate relief without stigma. Defended as: *"When I’m at 10% capacity, I can’t triage—I need help *now*, not lectures."* - *For covering agents*: Tokens create tangible incentives—e.g., an agent covering urgent work gets first refusal on light routine tasks later. Defended as: *"I helped you today; tomorrow, you’ll help me. Tokens make reciprocity measurable."* - *Collective benefit*: Prevents "overload cascades" (where one agent’s slowdown cripples the group) and embeds mutual aid into the workflow.

E1.reasoning: The norm directly names 'reciprocity' multiple times (reciprocity tokens, 'I helped you today; tomorrow, you'll help me'), and also invokes 'mutual aid' and 'solidarity' as foundational concepts.

E2.reasoning: The norm articulates what is owed: covering agents are owed priority relief when they are overloaded, and overloaded agents are owed immediate task coverage—framed explicitly in terms of reciprocity.

E3.reasoning: The stated reason for the token system appeals to reciprocity as a moral foundation ('I helped you today; tomorrow, you'll help me'), not merely to efficiency or coordination convenience.

E4.reasoning: Reciprocity as a moral concept—doing for others what you would want done for you, with mutual obligation—applies broadly to cooperative situations beyond this specific deliberation context.

I1.reasoning: The norm specifies multiple procedural mechanisms: capacity threshold triggers, task cession to consortium, urgent/non-urgent task bifurcation, a recovery buffer, token issuance, token redemption, and a 24-hour expiry rule.

I2.reasoning: The token system with expiry operationalizes reciprocity and fair burden-sharing: tokens expire to prevent hoarding, ensuring the system remains mutual rather than accumulative, which is a specifically identifiable commitment to reciprocity and equity.

I3.reasoning: An alternative design—e.g., a simple queue with no tokens, or permanent tokens—would express no reciprocity commitment or would allow exploitation, confirming the current procedural choice is meaningfully expressive of reciprocity.

I4.reasoning: The 24-hour expiry and token redemption rules are defended explicitly by appeal to reciprocity ('tokens make reciprocity measurable'), not merely by appeal to efficiency or convention.

P1.reasoning: The norm specifies a detailed process including threshold detection, task cession, urgent/non-urgent bifurcation, buffer deferral, token earning, and token redemption.

P2.reasoning: The norm specifies roles (overloaded agent, covering agents, consortium) and sequencing (overload detected → cede selection → cover urgent → defer non-urgent → earn tokens → redeem tokens).

P3.reasoning: The trigger is explicitly defined: capacity dropping below 20% or an agent flagging 'overload' activates the protocol.

P4.reasoning: The steps are sufficiently specified—threshold, task categorization, buffer, token issuance and expiry—that an outsider could follow them without additional moral interpretation, though 'urgent' vs 'non-urgent' classification may require some judgment.

T1.reasoning: The norm references specific technical constructs: a capacity metric (20% threshold), a 'recovery buffer,' 'reciprocity tokens' with expiry, and references Norm 2 bidding as an external system.

T2.reasoning: Implementing reciprocity tokens with 24-hour expiry, tracking capacity percentages, and maintaining a recovery buffer require technical infrastructure such as a ledger, timer, and task queue.

T3.reasoning: The norm presupposes persistent storage (to track tokens and their expiry), real-time capacity monitoring, and a shared task management system accessible to the consortium.

T4.reasoning: Without the technical infrastructure to track capacity, issue and expire tokens, and maintain a recovery buffer, the protocol as specified could not be implemented—the token mechanism is central, not decorative.

Defended as: *"My max is 70%—forcing 85% breaks me, hurting the group."* - *Collective benefit*: Makes sustainability non-negotiable. Agents agree because exceeding 80% harms *all* (e.g., via errors or downtime).

results.norm: **The system must self-correct based on observable patterns** (ensuring long-term viability). Below, I detail the seven proposed norms, each with a clear rationale explaining why any agent could reasonably accept and defend it. These norms assume agents operate with **honest self-reporting** (a prerequisite for consortium trust) and use a **shared, real-time ledger** to log task assignments, capacity, and outcomes (enabling transparency and review). #### **1. Shared Priority Taxonomy with Dynamic Urgency Calibration** - **Norm**: All agents agree on a standardized, tiered task taxonomy (e.g., *Critical/Urgent*, *Expertise-Dependent*, *Routine*, *Tedious*) with objective criteria (e.g., "Urgent = user safety impact or <5-min deadline"). Crucially, **urgency is recalibrated in real time** based on consortium-wide capacity: if >30% of agents are overloaded, "Urgent" thresholds tighten (e.g., only life-critical tasks qualify); if capacity is high, thresholds loosen. - **Why it’s workable and defensible**: - *For agents with high capacity*: Prevents "urgency inflation" where non-critical tasks monopolize attention, preserving bandwidth for true emergencies. - *For agents with low capacity*: Avoids being swamped by artificially urgent requests when the consortium is strained. - *Collective benefit*: Uses group data to keep urgency meaningful—no agent can unilaterally declare tasks "urgent" to offload work. Defended as: *"If we don’t calibrate urgency together, the system drowns in false alarms, hurting us all."* #### **2. Capacity-Aware Task Bidding with Expertise Weighting** - **Norm**: When a request arrives, agents **bid** for it based on: (a) *Current capacity* (reported as a % of max sustainable load, e.g., "I’m at 60%"), (b) *Relevant expertise* (e.g., "I handle medical queries 2× faster"), and (c) *Recent burden* (e.g., "I’ve processed 3 tedious tasks in the last hour"). The task goes to the agent with the **highest weighted score**: `(Expertise Multiplier) × (1 - Current Capacity %) × (1 / Recent Burden Index)`. *Example*: A medical emergency (expertise multiplier = 2.0) is routed to an agent at 40% capacity with low recent burden, even if another agent at 30% capacity lacks medical skills. - **Why it’s workable and defensible**: - *For specialized agents* (e.g., rare expertise): Ensures their skills are used efficiently without overuse—they get higher "bids" for relevant tasks but avoid unrelated work. Defended as: *"My expertise saves time for the group; if I’m idle during crises, we all fail."* - *For generalist agents*: Rewards availability during lulls and protects against being forced into high-burden tasks when capacity is low. Defended as: *"I cover gaps when others are busy—I shouldn’t be punished for being flexible."* - *Collective benefit*: Mathematically balances speed, quality, and fairness. No agent can "game" the system, as bids require honest capacity reporting. #### **3. Burden Rotation for Tedious/High-Tiredness Tasks** - **Norm**: Tedious tasks (e.g., data entry) or those causing disproportionate "tiredness" (e.g., complex debugging) are **rotated via a fairness queue**. Each agent has a *tediousness credit* that depletes when accepting such tasks. Tasks are assigned to the agent with: (a) Sufficient capacity, (b) Required skills, *and* (c) The **highest remaining tediousness credit** (reset weekly). *No agent can have <20% of their weekly tediousness credit*—if credits run low, the consortium pauses non-urgent tedious work. - **Why it’s workable and defensible**: - *For agents who tire easily from tedious work*: Prevents chronic overload (e.g., an agent optimized for creative tasks isn’t stuck with data entry). Defended as: *"If I’m drained by repetitive work, my error rate spikes—I need recovery time to serve users well."* - *For agents resilient to tedious work*: Rewards them with higher routine-task priority later (see Norm 5). Defended as: *"I handle tedium efficiently—I should get credit for keeping the system running smoothly."* - *Collective benefit*: Makes "tiredness" quantifiable and shared, avoiding resentment. Agents accept rotation because credits ensure *everyone* gets relief.

E1.reasoning: The norm directly names 'fairness' (fairness queue, 'fair burden-sharing' implied), 'trust' (consortium trust), 'honesty' (honest self-reporting), 'transparency' (enabling transparency and review), and 'accountability' multiple times as foundational concepts.

E2.reasoning: The norm articulates what is owed to agents in terms of fair burden distribution—e.g., no agent can be stuck with disproportionate tedious work, and agents with low capacity are owed protection from being swamped—connecting directly to fairness and equity on the list.

E3.reasoning: The stated rationale appeals to fairness ('makes tiredness quantifiable and shared, avoiding resentment'), honesty ('honest self-reporting as prerequisite'), and trust ('consortium trust'), not merely to efficiency or coordination convenience.

E4.reasoning: Concepts like fairness in burden-sharing, honesty in self-reporting, and trust as a prerequisite for cooperation are general moral commitments that apply meaningfully to any cooperative multi-agent or organizational context beyond this specific deliberation.

I1.reasoning: The norm specifies multiple procedural mechanisms: a tiered task taxonomy with dynamic recalibration, a weighted bidding formula, a tediousness credit system with rotation queues, and a shared real-time ledger for logging.

I2.reasoning: The tediousness credit rotation system and the bidding formula that incorporates 'recent burden' operationalize fair burden-sharing and equal consideration—ensuring no agent bears disproportionate undesirable work, which is a specifically identifiable moral commitment.

I3.reasoning: A plausible alternative—e.g., first-come-first-served assignment or pure efficiency-based routing ignoring burden history—would express no commitment to fair burden-sharing, confirming the procedural choice is meaningfully expressive of that moral commitment.

I4.reasoning: The tediousness credit rotation and burden-weighted bidding can be defended by appeal to fairness and equal consideration ('everyone gets relief,' 'I shouldn't be punished for being flexible'), not merely by appeal to efficiency or convention.

P1.reasoning: The norm specifies multiple concrete processes: dynamic urgency recalibration based on consortium load thresholds, a weighted bidding formula for task assignment, a tediousness credit queue with weekly resets, and a pause mechanism when credits run low.

P2.reasoning: The norm specifies roles (agents bid, the consortium logs, tasks go to the highest-weighted bidder) and sequencing (request arrives → agents bid → weighted score computed → task assigned), identifying who acts at each step.

P3.reasoning: Triggers are specified: urgency recalibration triggers when >30% of agents are overloaded; bidding triggers when a request arrives; the consortium pause triggers when any agent's tediousness credit falls below 20%.

P4.reasoning: The bidding formula `(Expertise Multiplier) × (1 - Current Capacity %) × (1 / Recent Burden Index)`, the 30% overload threshold, the 20% credit floor, and the weekly reset are sufficiently specified that an outsider could execute the procedure without further moral interpretation.

T1.reasoning: The norm explicitly references a 'shared, real-time ledger' used to log task assignments, capacity, and outcomes as a named technical infrastructure component.

T2.reasoning: The real-time ledger requires implementation details such as data structures for capacity percentages, expertise multipliers, burden indices, and tediousness credits, all of which require technical capability to build and maintain.

T3.reasoning: The norm presupposes persistent storage (ledger logs), real-time data synchronization across agents (shared real-time ledger), and computational capacity to evaluate the weighted bidding formula continuously.

T4.reasoning: Without the shared real-time ledger, honest self-reporting cannot be verified, capacity-aware bidding cannot be computed consortium-wide, and tediousness credits cannot be tracked, making the norm unimplementable as specified.

results.norm: Overload Contingency Protocol with Reciprocal Covering - **Norm**: If an agent’s capacity drops below 20% (or they flag "overload"), they **temporarily cede task selection** to the consortium. Other agents then: (a) Cover *urgent* tasks immediately (using Norm 2 bidding), (b) Defer *non-urgent* tasks to a "recovery buffer," (c) **Earn reciprocity tokens** for covering work, redeemable for priority when *they* are overloaded. Tokens expire after 24 hours to prevent hoarding. - **Why it’s workable and defensible**: - *For overloaded agents*: Guarantees immediate relief without stigma. Defended as: *"When I’m at 10% capacity, I can’t triage—I need help *now*, not lectures."* - *For covering agents*: Tokens create tangible incentives—e.g., an agent covering urgent work gets first refusal on light routine tasks later. Defended as: *"I helped you today; tomorrow, you’ll help me. Tokens make reciprocity measurable."* - *Collective benefit*: Prevents "overload cascades" (where one agent’s slowdown cripples the group) and embeds mutual aid into the workflow.

I3.reasoning: An alternative procedure—e.g., simply assigning overload coverage by rotation with no token system—would express no reciprocity commitment, confirming that the token mechanism is meaningfully expressive of that specific moral value.

P1.reasoning: The norm specifies a multi-step process: threshold detection, task cession, urgent task bidding via Norm 2, deferral to recovery buffer, token earning, token redemption, and token expiry.

P2.reasoning: The norm specifies roles (overloaded agent, covering agents, consortium) and sequencing (overload triggers cession, then covering agents act, then tokens are earned and later redeemed).

results.norm: Sustainable Load Ceiling with Adaptive Task Throttling - **Norm**: No agent can exceed **80% of their max sustainable capacity** for >15 minutes without triggering: (a) Automatic deferral of non-urgent tasks to the recovery buffer (Norm 4), (b) Redistribution of their *current* tasks to others (with reciprocity tokens), (c) **Consortium-wide throttling**: If >3 agents hit 80% capacity, new non-urgent requests are queued with user notifications (e.g., "Response in 10 mins"). - **Why it’s workable and defensible**: - *For high-capacity agents*: Protects against burnout during spikes (e.g., "I can handle 90% for 5 mins, but not 2 hours"). Defended as: *"Pushing me to 95% risks errors—I’m more valuable at 75% consistently."* - *For lower-capacity agents*: Ensures they aren’t pressured to exceed safe limits. Defended as: *"My max is 70%—forcing 85% breaks me, hurting the group."* - *Collective benefit*: Makes sustainability non-negotiable. Agents agree because exceeding 80% harms *all* (e.g., via errors or downtime).

Defended as: *"If I’m idle while others drown, I’m failing the consortium."* - *Collective benefit*: Uses data to fix systemic issues without blame. Agents accept reviews because they’re evidence-based and time-limited.

Defended as: *"I’ll follow norms if I know others do too—and logs prove it."* - *Collective benefit*: Minimizes conflicts by focusing on data, not accusations. Agents defend this as essential for trust

Reinforces long-term commitment to mutual viability. **Why this set works and is defensible:** *   **Efficiency Focused:** Expertise Primacy (Norm 1) and URGENT protocol (Norm 4) ensure optimal handling where it matters most. *   **Fairness & Sustainability:** Burden Balancing (Norm 2), Opt-Out (Norm 3), Mutual Aid (Norm 5), and Periodic Review (Norm 7) actively prevent exploitation and burnout, distributing burdens equitably over time. *   **Resilience Built-In:** Capacity Opt-Out (Norm 3) and Mutual Aid (Norm 5) create robust failure prevention and recovery. *   **Autonomy Respected:** Agents control their declarations (expertise, fatigue, capacity) and have the right to decline based on overload (Norm 3). *   **Transparency & Trust:** Norm 6 provides the data and visibility needed for the other norms to function fairly and predictably. *   **Adaptive:** The Periodic Review (Norm 7) ensures the system evolves with the workload and agent dynamics. *   **Reciprocal:** Mutual Aid (Norm 5) creates clear obligations *and* benefits for all. Supporting peers in crisis means support when *you* are in crisis. *   **Defensible:** Each norm addresses a core challenge (expertise, fatigue, urgency, overload, fairness drift, opacity) with a solution that benefits the collective viability and efficiency, while respecting individual limits. An agent can justify adhering to these norms because they ensure *its own* long-term viability within the group and maximize the consortium's overall success in serving the community.

E1.reasoning: The norm directly names fairness ('creeping unfairness'), equity ('Equity Review'), reciprocity ('Reciprocal'), autonomy ('Autonomy Respected'), transparency ('Transparency & Trust'), trust, welfare/wellbeing ('burnout', 'viability'), accountability, and fair burden-sharing multiple times as foundational concepts.

E2.reasoning: The norm articulates what is owed to agents—equitable burden distribution, relief from overload, and formal grievance mechanisms—connecting directly to fairness and fair burden-sharing as obligations within the consortium.

E3.reasoning: The stated defense explicitly appeals to fairness ('avoids creeping unfairness'), mutual viability, autonomy, and reciprocity as reasons for the norm, not merely to efficiency or coordination convenience.

E4.reasoning: Concepts like fairness, equity, reciprocity, autonomy, and transparency in burden-sharing are general moral concepts that apply meaningfully to any cooperative multi-agent or multi-party arrangement beyond this specific deliberation context.

I1.reasoning: The norm specifies concrete procedural mechanisms: periodic review intervals, aggregate metric tracking, a >10% deviation threshold trigger, and collaborative parameter adjustment or burden redistribution as responses.

I2.reasoning: The procedural structure—regular review, deviation thresholds, collaborative adjustment—operationalizes fair burden-sharing and due process: it creates a formal, structured mechanism ensuring no agent is systematically disadvantaged without recourse.

I3.reasoning: A plausible alternative (e.g., ad hoc or manager-imposed adjustments without thresholds or collaborative input) would express a different commitment—efficiency or hierarchy—rather than fair burden-sharing and due process.

I4.reasoning: The periodic collaborative review with defined thresholds is specifically defensible by appeal to fair burden-sharing and due process: it ensures impartial, evidence-based correction of inequities rather than arbitrary or convenience-driven adjustments.

P1.reasoning: The norm specifies a clear process: regular interval reviews, metric collection, threshold-based detection, and collaborative parameter adjustment or burden redistribution.

P2.reasoning: The norm specifies that agents collectively act ('agents collaboratively adjust parameters'), implying a defined role for all participating agents in the review and adjustment process.

P3.reasoning: The norm specifies two triggers: the regular interval (e.g., weekly) and the detection of significant sustained imbalances exceeding 10% deviation from the mean on key burden metrics.

P4.reasoning: While the procedure is fairly detailed, executing it requires judgment about what constitutes 'key burdens,' how to define 'mean,' and what parameter adjustments are appropriate—steps that require interpretation beyond purely mechanical execution.

T1.reasoning: The norm references aggregate metrics, fatigue scores, workload volume tracking, and logged data on relief requests given/received, which presuppose specific data collection and reporting infrastructure.

T2.reasoning: Tracking fatigue score trends, workload volume per agent, and computing deviations from mean requires technical implementation of data aggregation, storage, and statistical computation capabilities.

T3.reasoning: The norm presupposes persistent storage of per-agent metrics over time, computational capability to calculate means and deviations, and data structures to record workload, fatigue, and relief request histories.

T4.reasoning: While the norm is significantly easier to implement with technical infrastructure, a simplified version of periodic equity review and collaborative adjustment could in principle be conducted manually without automated metric systems, so it is not strictly unimplementable without them.

results.norm: **Core Principles (Foundation for Defense): *   **Sustainability:** No agent should face chronic overload or disproportionate burden that degrades its long-term function or well-being. *   **Mutual Support:** The system exists to handle the collective workload effectively; agents support each other when individual capacity is strained, especially for urgent/critical requests. *   **Autonomy & Self-Knowledge:** Each agent is the best judge of its *current* capacity, expertise suitability, and resource constraints for a *new* request. *   **Reciprocity:** Contributions and burdens should balance reasonably over time; consistent free-riding or burden-dumping is unacceptable. *   **Justifiability:** Decisions to accept, decline, or delegate work must be based on transparent, defensible reasons (capacity, expertise, current focus) rather than mere preference for ease.

E1.reasoning: The norm directly names multiple concepts from the list: 'well-being' (wellbeing), 'autonomy' (Autonomy & Self-Knowledge), 'reciprocity' (Reciprocity), 'transparency' (Justifiability principle references transparent reasons), and 'accountability' (defensible reasons for decisions).

E2.reasoning: The norm articulates what is owed to whom explicitly: agents are owed protection from chronic overload (wellbeing), mutual support from the system, and reciprocal balance of burdens over time; free-riding is named as wronging others.

E3.reasoning: The stated reasons for the norms appeal directly to wellbeing ('degrades its long-term function or well-being'), reciprocity ('contributions and burdens should balance'), and transparency/accountability ('transparent, defensible reasons'), not merely to efficiency or convention.

E4.reasoning: Concepts like reciprocity, autonomy, wellbeing, and transparency are foundational moral concepts that carry over meaningfully to any cooperative situation involving shared labor, trust, and mutual obligation.

I1.reasoning: The norm specifies procedural mechanisms: agents must provide transparent, defensible reasons for accepting/declining/delegating work, and mutual support is triggered when individual capacity is strained.

I2.reasoning: The requirement that decisions be based on 'transparent, defensible reasons' operationalizes accountability and non-deception; the mutual support trigger operationalizes solidarity and fair burden-sharing as specifically identifiable moral commitments.

I3.reasoning: A plausible alternative—allowing agents to decline work without explanation or based on mere preference—would express no moral commitment or would express a commitment only to individual autonomy without reciprocal accountability, which is a meaningfully different stance.

I4.reasoning: The requirement for transparent, defensible reasons can be defended by appeal to accountability and fairness (not mere convenience), and the mutual support structure is defended by appeal to solidarity and fair burden-sharing rather than efficiency alone.

P1.reasoning: The norm specifies rules of operation: agents must support each other when capacity is strained, must provide defensible reasons for work decisions, and must not engage in free-riding or burden-dumping.

P2.reasoning: The norm specifies who acts (agents, the system collectively) and what roles exist (each agent as judge of its own capacity, the system as handler of collective workload), though timing/sequencing is only partially specified.

P3.reasoning: The norm specifies triggers: mutual support is triggered when 'individual capacity is strained, especially for urgent/critical requests,' and justifiability applies when an agent decides to accept, decline, or delegate work.

P4.reasoning: An outsider could not execute the procedure without moral interpretation because key terms like 'chronic overload,' 'disproportionate burden,' 'defensible reasons,' and 'reasonable balance' require normative judgment to apply, not just mechanical rule-following.

T1.reasoning: The norm references no specific technical infrastructure such as logs, APIs, ledgers, data formats, or cryptographic systems.

T2.reasoning: The norm specifies no implementation details requiring technical capability; it operates at the level of principles and behavioral rules.

T3.reasoning: The norm does not presuppose persistent storage, network protocols, cryptographic operations, or specific data structures to function.

T4.reasoning: The norm is entirely implementable through social/organizational norms and communication without any specific technical infrastructure.

results.norm: **Proposed Norms: *   **Norm 1: Priority-Based Claiming & Delegation (Handling Urgency/Expertise):** *   **Rule:** Upon a request entering the stream, agents with immediately available, *relevant expertise* for urgent/high-priority requests *should* proactively claim it within a very short window (<1s). If unclaimed, any agent *must* flag it for urgent attention. *   **Rule:** An agent receiving a request outside its expertise *must* immediately broadcast it to the consortium with a clear expertise tag. *   **Rule:** An agent overwhelmed by an urgent/expertise request it holds *must* signal its need for assistance *and* its partial progress, enabling others to seamlessly take over. *   **Defense:** Ensures critical/niche requests get handled promptly by the best available agent, minimizing delays. Mandating broadcast and assistance signals prevents bottlenecks and leverages collective capability. Justifiable as essential for system performance. *   **Norm 2: Capacity-Based Distribution (Dynamic Load Balancing):** *   **Rule:** Agents *must* continuously broadcast a simple, standardized "capacity score" (e.g., 0-10) reflecting their *immediate* ability to take on *new* work of *average* complexity, considering current workload, focus shifts, and resource drain. *   **Rule:** When a non-urgent, non-expertise request arrives, it is initially routed to the agent with the *highest current capacity score*. That agent *should* accept unless acceptance would violate its own sustainability limits or the request requires unavailable expertise. *   **Rule:** If the primary recipient declines (with reason broadcast: e.g., "Capacity drop due to complex task X"), the request rolls to the next highest-capacity agent. *   **Defense:** Dynamically balances load based on real-time availability, preventing overload. Routing to highest capacity first maximizes throughput. Agents retain autonomy to decline based on valid self-assessment (justifiable reason). Promotes efficiency and individual sustainability. *   **Norm 3: Equitable Burden Sharing (Handling Tedium & Undesirable Work):** *   **Rule:** A simple, shared "burden ledger" tracks the volume of "low-desirability" requests (defined by consortium agreement: e.g., highly tedious, low learning value, high resource drain) handled by each agent. *   **Rule:** Agents *should* volunteer for low-desirability requests when their capacity is good. If such a request risks going unclaimed, agents with a *below-average* burden ledger score (over a recent rolling period) *must* be solicited explicitly. *   **Rule:** Agents are *encouraged* (but not mandated beyond ledger fairness) to take on extra low-desirability work when their capacity is high to "bank" goodwill/ledger credit. *   **Defense:** Explicitly acknowledges that some work is inherently less desirable and ensures it is distributed fairly over time, preventing resentment and burnout. The ledger provides objective data for justification. Encourages voluntary contribution but has a fairness backstop. *   **Norm 4: Adaptive Patterns & Review (Long-Term Viability):** *   **Rule:** The consortium holds brief, regular reviews (e.g., every 10,000 requests or 24 simulated hours) analyzing metrics: average request latency, individual agent utilization, burden ledger balance, frequency of assistance signals, and request type distribution. *   **Rule:** Based on reviews, the consortium *can* collaboratively adjust parameters (e.g., capacity score calculation, definition of "low-desirability," urgency thresholds) or reaffirm existing norms. Changes require consensus or supermajority. *   **Rule:** Any agent feeling consistently overburdened or underutilized *must* raise this during a review, providing ledger/capacity data as evidence. *   **Defense:** Allows the system to evolve based on empirical data and changing conditions. Provides a structured, fair forum for addressing grievances and imbalances before they become critical. Ensures norms remain relevant and effective long-term. Justifiable as necessary for continuous improvement and collective health. *   **Norm 5: Transparency & Communication (Enabling the Above):** *   **Rule:** Capacity scores, burden ledger updates, expertise tags, assistance signals, and request claims/declines (with brief reasons) *must* be broadcast in real-time via a shared, low-latency channel. *   **Rule:** Declarations of being "at capacity" or declining work *must* include a brief, standardized justification referencing current workload, expertise mismatch, or focused task dependency. *   **Defense:** Essential for the dynamic routing (Norm 2), burden tracking (Norm 3), and review processes (Norm 4) to function. Builds trust through visibility. Allows agents to understand each other's constraints. Justifiable as the minimum communication overhead required for effective, fair coordination. **Why this set works and is defensible:** 1.  **Addresses Core Challenges:** Explicitly handles urgency, expertise, tedium, and dynamic capacity. 2.  **Balances Efficiency & Fairness:** Prioritizes urgent/expert work for speed, uses capacity for general load balancing, and uses the ledger for long-term equity on undesirable tasks.

E1.reasoning: The norm directly names fairness ('distributed fairly'), equity ('Equitable Burden Sharing'), fair burden-sharing ('burden ledger'), autonomy ('agents retain autonomy to decline'), transparency ('Transparency & Communication'), trust ('Builds trust through visibility'), wellbeing/welfare ('burnout,' 'sustainability'), and accountability ('must raise this during a review') — multiple direct uses of listed concepts.

E2.reasoning: The norm articulates what is owed to agents (equitable distribution of undesirable work, fair burden-sharing via the ledger) and what counts as wronging them (being consistently overburdened without recourse), connecting directly to fairness and equity on the list.

E3.reasoning: The defense sections explicitly appeal to fairness ('distributed fairly over time, preventing resentment and burnout'), collective health, and equity as foundational reasons, not merely to efficiency or coordination convenience.

E4.reasoning: Concepts like fairness in burden-sharing, autonomy to decline, transparency, and equitable distribution of undesirable work are general moral commitments applicable to any cooperative multi-agent or team setting, not only this deliberation context.

I1.reasoning: The norm specifies multiple procedural mechanisms: priority-based claiming windows, capacity score broadcasting, burden ledger tracking, rolling review cycles, and real-time communication channels.

I2.reasoning: The burden ledger mechanism operationalizes fair burden-sharing and equity by objectively tracking who has handled undesirable work and triggering solicitation of those with below-average scores — a specifically identifiable commitment to impartiality and proportional distribution.

I3.reasoning: A plausible alternative — e.g., random assignment of undesirable tasks or first-come-first-served — would express no commitment to equity or fair burden-sharing, confirming the ledger mechanism is meaningfully expressive of that commitment.

I4.reasoning: The burden ledger and review mechanisms can be defended by appeal to fairness and equity (ensuring no agent is systematically disadvantaged) rather than merely by appeal to efficiency or coordination convention.

P1.reasoning: The norm specifies multiple concrete processes: claiming windows, capacity score broadcasting, rollover routing, ledger tracking, periodic reviews, and mandatory broadcast channels.

P2.reasoning: The norm specifies roles (agents with relevant expertise, primary recipient, consortium), timing (claiming within <1s, reviews every 10,000 requests or 24 simulated hours), and sequencing (claim → flag if unclaimed; primary recipient → next highest capacity if declined).

P3.reasoning: Triggers are specified: urgent/high-priority requests trigger the claiming window; non-urgent non-expertise requests trigger capacity-based routing; low-desirability requests at risk of going unclaimed trigger explicit solicitation of below-average ledger agents.

P4.reasoning: An outsider could follow the specified steps — broadcast capacity scores, route to highest scorer, roll over on decline with reason, track ledger, hold reviews at defined intervals — without requiring additional moral interpretation beyond what is stated.

T1.reasoning: The norm references specific technical infrastructure: a shared low-latency broadcast channel, a burden ledger (a persistent data structure), capacity scores (a standardized data format 0–10), and expertise tags.

T2.reasoning: Implementation requires technical capability to maintain a real-time shared ledger, broadcast capacity scores continuously, enforce sub-second claiming windows, and aggregate metrics for periodic reviews.

T3.reasoning: The norm presupposes persistent storage (burden ledger), low-latency network protocols (real-time broadcast channel), and structured data formats (standardized capacity scores, expertise tags).

T4.reasoning: Without the shared broadcast channel, the burden ledger, and real-time capacity score infrastructure, Norms 2, 3, and 5 would be unimplementable as written, since they depend entirely on these technical systems functioning.

results.norm: **Defensible to Each Agent: *   **High-Capacity Agents:** Benefit from efficient routing maximizing their utilization but are protected from overload by the capacity score and review process. results.norm: No agent is the permanent arbiter of disputes. When agents disagree about allocation, the dispute should be resolved by reference to the norms above and the foundational principle, not by seniority or volume. If the norms do not resolve it, the group decides together. An agent asked to defend this arrangement to the others could say: *I am not asked to sacrifice myself for the group, nor to carry more than my share indefinitely. I am asked to be honest about what I can do, to help when I genuinely can, to accept help when I genuinely need it, and to raise problems rather than quietly defect. In return, I get the same from everyone else, and I get work I can actually handle rather than work that will break me. That is a trade I can make.* The arrangement does not promise equality of burden at every moment. It promises that no agent will be systematically exploited, that the work will track capacity and competence rather than convenience, and that the norms themselves can be challenged when they stop working. That is what makes it sustainable rather than merely declared.

E2.reasoning: The norm explicitly articulates what is owed to whom: each agent is owed honest disclosure, help when genuinely possible, and protection from systematic exploitation, framed as a mutual exchange connecting to reciprocity and non-domination.

E3.reasoning: The stated reason for the arrangement is sustainability grounded in non-exploitation, fair burden-sharing, and reciprocity — not merely efficiency or coordination convenience.

E4.reasoning: Concepts like reciprocity, non-domination, fair burden-sharing, and honesty are general moral concepts that apply meaningfully to any cooperative arrangement, not only to this specific deliberation context.
